name: Argentina
audioHint: Like 'argue' + 'een' + 'ah' — stress on the third syllable
funFact: Argentina's name comes from the Latin 'argentum' (silver) — Spanish explorers believed the region had vast silver deposits. They were wrong about the silver but right about the football talent.

name: Australia
audioHint: Aussies say it with the 'o' in 'odd' — oz-TRAY-lee-uh, not aw-STRAY-lee-uh
funFact: Australia call themselves the 'Socceroos' — a blend of soccer and kangaroo. Their national team badge literally features a kangaroo.

name: Austria
audioHint: Three syllables, stress on the first — OS-tree-uh. Not Australia!
funFact: Austria and Australia are so often confused that Vienna's tourist board once ran a campaign clarifying: 'no kangaroos in Austria.'

name: Belgium
audioHint: Short and punchy — BEL-jum. The 'gi' makes a 'j' sound.
funFact: Belgium has three official languages (Dutch, French, German), so 'Belgium' sounds different depending on which region you're in — Belgiё, Belgique, or Belgien.

name: Bolivia
audioHint: Stress the 'LIV' — boh-LIV-ee-uh. Named after liberator Simón Bolívar.
funFact: Bolivia has the world's highest international football stadium. La Paz's Estadio Hernando Siles sits at 3,600m altitude — visiting teams often struggle to breathe, let alone play.

name: Bosnia & Herzegovina
audioHint: 'Bosnia' is the easy bit — BOZ-nee-uh. 'Herzegovina' breaks down as HERT-seh-GOV-ih-nah. The 'z' in Herzegovina sounds like 'ts'.
funFact: Herzegovina takes its name from 'Herzog' (German for duke) — the region was once ruled by a medieval duke who styled himself 'Duke of Saint Sava.'

name: Brazil
audioHint: Stress on the second syllable — bruh-ZIL. In Portuguese: bruh-ZEEL.
funFact: Brazil is the only country to have qualified for every single FIFA World Cup since the tournament began in 1930. They've won it a record five times.

name: Cameroon
audioHint: Three syllables, rhymes with 'afternoon' — KAM-uh-ROON.
funFact: Cameroon is called 'Africa in miniature' — the country has beaches, deserts, rainforests, and mountains. Their football team, the Indomitable Lions, made history as the first African team to reach a World Cup quarter-final (1990).

name: Canada
audioHint: KAN-uh-duh. The last two syllables are unstressed schwa sounds.
funFact: Canada co-hosts the 2026 World Cup. Despite being home to millions of immigrants from football-mad nations, the sport only rose to major prominence in Canada after their historic 2022 World Cup qualification — their first since 1986.

name: Chile
audioHint: In English: CHIL-ee (like the pepper). In Spanish: CHEE-leh — two distinct syllables with equal stress.
funFact: Chile's name origin is disputed — it may come from the indigenous Mapuche word 'chili' meaning 'where the land ends,' or from a bird's call. Their 1962 World Cup was played in their own country during a national earthquake recovery.

name: Colombia
audioHint: Note the spelling — it's Colombia, NOT Columbia. Stress the 'LUM': kuh-LUM-bee-uh.
funFact: Always spell it Colombia with an 'o' — never Columbia (that's the university and river). The country is named after explorer Christopher Columbus.

name: Costa Rica
audioHint: Spanish pronunciation: KOHS-tah REE-kah — stress on REE. It means 'Rich Coast.'
funFact: Costa Rica stunned the football world at the 2014 World Cup, reaching the quarter-finals after defeating Uruguay, Italy, and England — finishing top of their group.

name: Côte d'Ivoire
audioHint: French: the 'ô' is a long 'o', and 'Ivoire' ends with a 'v-war' sound — koht dee-VWAR. The English 'Ivory Coast' is also acceptable.
funFact: FIFA requires all official match documents to use 'Côte d'Ivoire' rather than 'Ivory Coast.' The country officially requested this in 1986 to preserve their French name worldwide.
localPronunciation: Côte d'Ivoire = koht dee-VWAHR (French, final 'r' is throaty/uvular)

name: Croatia
audioHint: Three syllables — kroh-AY-shuh. The local name is 'Hrvatska' (HURV-aht-skah) — completely different!
funFact: Croatia's local name 'Hrvatska' is where the word 'cravat' (necktie) originates — Croatian soldiers in 17th century France wore distinctive scarves around their necks which became a Parisian fashion craze.

name: Curaçao
audioHint: The 'ç' (cedilla) makes a soft 's' sound — not 'k' or 'ch'. Say: kyoo-RAH-soh. Think: 'cure-ah-so.'
funFact: Curaçao is a Caribbean island the size of Atlanta, but has produced top-flight players across Europe. The blue Curaçao liqueur is made from the dried peel of the laraha orange, which only grows on the island.
localPronunciation: Kòrsou = KOR-sow (Papiamentu, the local creole language)

name: Czechia
audioHint: The 'Cz' at the start makes a 'ch' sound — CHEK-ee-uh. Also known as the Czech Republic.
funFact: Czechia is the country's official short-form name since 2016, though 'Czech Republic' is still widely used. The local name starts with 'Č' — a 'ch' sound — Česká republika.

name: Denmark
audioHint: Straightforward — DEN-mark. The Danes say DAN-mark (with a softer 'a').
funFact: Denmark's 'Danish Dynamite' team of the 1980s is still considered one of the most entertaining international sides ever. They qualified for their first World Cup in 1986 and immediately stunned the world.

name: Ecuador
audioHint: Stress on the first syllable — EK-wuh-dor. It's named after the equator ('Ecuador' = equator in Spanish), which passes directly through the country.
funFact: Ecuador sits on the equator — the country's name literally means 'equator.' Their capital Quito is one of the highest capitals in the world at 2,850 metres altitude.

name: Egypt
audioHint: EE-jipt — the 'gy' makes a 'j' sound. In Arabic, Egypt is simply 'Misr' (MIS-r).
funFact: Egyptians call their country 'Misr' — one of the oldest place names still in use. Ancient Egyptians played ball games going back thousands of years; some historians consider Egypt a birthplace of ball sports.

name: England
audioHint: ING-glund — note the 'ng' sound blends into 'gl'. Not 'Ing-land' with two clear syllables.
funFact: England invented the modern rules of association football in 1863 when the Football Association was founded. They've been waiting to win the World Cup again since their only triumph in 1966.

name: France
audioHint: One syllable with a broad 'ah' vowel — FRAHNS. The French pronounce it with a nasal 'n': fʁɑ̃s.
funFact: France's national team is known as 'Les Bleus' (The Blues). With players from 18+ nationalities, France's 1998 World Cup squad was celebrated as a model of multicultural unity — 'black, blanc, beur.'
localPronunciation: France = fʁɑ̃s (French nasal vowel, no distinct 'n' at end)

name: Germany
audioHint: JER-muh-nee in English. The local name Deutschland is completely different: DOYTS-lant.
funFact: Germany is the only country whose national football team has won the World Cup under three different names: West Germany (1954, 1974, 1990) and Germany (2014).

name: Ghana
audioHint: GAH-nuh — the 'Gh' makes a hard 'G' sound. Stress on the first syllable.
funFact: Ghana's 'Black Stars' became the first African team to reach a World Cup semi-final when they nearly did so in 2010 — only denied by Luis Suárez's infamous handball on the goal line. Their name references the star on their flag.

name: Honduras
audioHint: The 'du' blends into a 'dyu' sound — hon-DYOOR-us. In Spanish: on-DOO-ras.
funFact: The 1969 'Football War' between Honduras and El Salvador was triggered partly by a heated World Cup qualifying match, though deep political tensions were the true cause. The conflict lasted 100 hours.

name: Iran
audioHint: ih-RAHN — stress on the second syllable with a broad 'ah'. NOT 'EYE-ran' which is considered dated and offensive.
funFact: Iran is one of Asia's most successful football nations, qualifying for multiple World Cups. In Farsi, the country is called 'Irân' — the long vowel is important. 'Eye-ran' dates back to Western mispronunciation.

name: Iraq
audioHint: ih-RAK — two syllables, stress on second. Not 'EYE-rack'.
funFact: Iraq's football association was founded in 1948. The country famously won the Asian Cup in 2007 during a period of intense internal conflict — the victory sparked nationwide celebrations.

name: Italy
audioHint: IT-uh-lee — stress firmly on the first syllable. In Italian: Italia = ee-TAH-lee-ah.
funFact: Italy (Gli Azzurri / The Blues) have won the World Cup four times. They famously failed to qualify for 2018, ending a 60-year run at the tournament — a shock felt across world football.

name: Jamaica
audioHint: juh-MAY-kuh — stress on the middle syllable. Easy once you stop second-guessing the 'J'.
funFact: Jamaica's 'Reggae Boyz' became the first Caribbean nation to qualify for the World Cup when they made it to France 1998. Their supporters brought Jamaican vibes — literally — to the tournament.

name: Japan
audioHint: juh-PAN. In Japanese the country is called 'Nihon' or 'Nippon' — ni-HON or NIP-pon.
funFact: Japan calls itself Nihon or Nippon (ni-HON/NIP-pon), meaning 'origin of the sun' — hence 'Land of the Rising Sun.' Japan has been to every World Cup since 1998 and their Blue Samurai always bring passionate, respectful fans.

name: Jordan
audioHint: JOR-dun — simple two syllables, stress on first.
funFact: Jordan's national team, the Nashama, is on a remarkable rise in Asian football. Their name 'Nashama' means 'brave' in Arabic.

name: Mexico
audioHint: MEK-sih-koh in English. In Spanish: MEH-hee-koh — the 'x' makes an 'h' sound!
funFact: In Spanish, Mexico is spelled 'México' and the 'x' is pronounced like an English 'h' — MEH-hee-koh. This is why Spanish-speakers sometimes write it as 'Méjico.' Mexico is a World Cup co-host for 2026, their third hosting stint.

name: Morocco
audioHint: muh-ROK-oh. Locally known as 'Al-Maghrib' (al-MAH-rib) meaning 'The West' — Morocco sits at the westernmost edge of the Arab world.
funFact: Morocco made history at the 2022 World Cup as the first African and Arab nation to reach the semi-finals. Their local name Al-Maghrib means 'the west' — since Morocco is the westernmost Arab country.

name: Netherlands
audioHint: NETH-er-lundz — the 'th' is soft as in 'the', not hard as in 'thing'. Also widely called Holland (though technically Holland refers to two provinces only).
funFact: The Netherlands officially rebranded from 'Holland' to 'Netherlands' in 2020 to reflect the whole country, not just its western provinces. Their 'Total Football' philosophy in the 1970s changed how the world thinks about the game.

name: New Zealand
audioHint: nyoo ZEE-lund — the Māori name Aotearoa (AH-oh-teh-ah-ROH-ah) is increasingly used alongside the English name.
funFact: New Zealand's Māori name is Aotearoa (AH-oh-teh-ah-ROH-ah), meaning 'land of the long white cloud.' Their football side, the All Whites, are the underdogs' underdogs — they've never lost a World Cup group stage match (but only played in two groups).

name: Nigeria
audioHint: ny-JEER-ee-uh — four syllables, stress on JEER. The 'Ni' sounds like 'nye' (eye).
funFact: Nigeria's Super Eagles are Africa's most successful World Cup nation by round reached. Their green-and-white kit designs are often voted among the most beloved in World Cup history.

name: Panama
audioHint: PAN-uh-mah in English. In Spanish: pah-nah-MAH — stress on the last syllable.
funFact: Panama made their World Cup debut at Russia 2018. When they scored against England — even as they were losing heavily — their commentator delivered one of the most celebrated commentaries in football history.

name: Paraguay
audioHint: PAIR-uh-gwy — the ending 'guay' is one syllable: 'gwy' (like 'guy'). In Spanish: pah-rah-GWAY.
funFact: Paraguay is one of only two landlocked countries in South America (along with Bolivia). Their name comes from the Guaraní language — one of the few countries where an indigenous language is co-official with Spanish.

name: Peru
audioHint: puh-ROO — stress on the second syllable. In Spanish: peh-ROO.
funFact: Peru went 36 years without a World Cup appearance before returning in 2018. Their fans, known for their passionate 'Blanquirroja' support, traveled en masse to Russia.

name: Poland
audioHint: POH-lund — the 'land' ending is reduced to 'lund'. In Polish: POLE-skah.
funFact: Poland's local name Polska comes from the Polans tribe, meaning 'people of the fields.' They produced Robert Lewandowski, widely considered one of the greatest strikers of his generation.

name: Portugal
audioHint: POR-chuh-gul — the 'tu' sounds like 'chu' in English. In Portuguese: por-too-GAHL.
funFact: In Portuguese, Portugal is pronounced por-too-GAHL with stress on the final syllable. The name may derive from Portus Cale, a Roman settlement near modern Porto.

name: Qatar
audioHint: The official English pronunciation is KAH-tar. The Arabic is more like 'GUTtar' with a 'q' that comes from deep in the throat. 'KAT-ar' (to rhyme with 'batter') is the most common mispronunciation.
funFact: Qatar's name in Arabic uses the letter Qāf (ق) — a deep throat consonant with no English equivalent. The BBC officially settled on 'KAH-tar' for 2022. Qatar hosted the first World Cup in the Middle East, moving the tournament to winter months for the first time.

name: Saudi Arabia
audioHint: SOW-dee uh-RAY-bee-uh — 'Saudi' rhymes with 'cloudy'. Stress on 'RAY' in Arabia.
funFact: Saudi Arabia stunned Argentina 2-1 at the 2022 World Cup in what many called the biggest upset in tournament history. They qualify regularly for the World Cup despite only joining FIFA in 1956.

name: Scotland
audioHint: SKOT-lund — short 'o' as in 'hot'. The Gaelic name is Alba (AL-uh-puh).
funFact: Scotland is one of football's founding nations — the first international match in history was England vs Scotland in 1872. Their Gaelic name, Alba, is used in the Scottish national anthem 'Flower of Scotland.'

name: Senegal
audioHint: SEN-ih-gawl — stress on the final syllable. The 'gal' sounds like 'gawl' (rhymes with 'ball').
funFact: Senegal's Lions of Teranga reached the quarter-finals in their World Cup debut in 2002 — beating reigning champions France in the opening game. They became Africa's first World Cup holders since 2019 after winning the AFCON.

name: South Africa
audioHint: sowth AF-ri-kuh — stress on AF. The country has 11 official languages with 11 different names for itself!
funFact: South Africa hosted the 2010 World Cup — the first on African soil. The vuvuzela horn, which buzzed through every match, became one of the most polarising — and unforgettable — sounds in World Cup history. The country has 11 official languages.

name: South Korea
audioHint: sowth kuh-REE-uh. Locals call it Han-guk (한국) — HAHN-gook — or Daehan-minguk for the formal name.
funFact: South Korea reached the semi-finals at the 2002 World Cup on home soil — defeating Spain, Italy, and Poland along the way. In Korean, the country is 대한민국 (Daehan Minguk) — 'Great Han Nation.'

name: Spain
audioHint: SPAYN — one syllable. España in Spanish: es-PAHN-yah.
funFact: España (es-PAHN-yah) — the Spanish name — features the 'ñ' (eñe) that English doesn't have. Spain's 'La Roja' (The Red) revolutionised football with tiki-taka possession play, winning the 2010 World Cup and two European Championships consecutively.

name: Switzerland
audioHint: SWIT-ser-lund. Switzerland has FOUR official languages — so four different local names. The German 'Schweiz' = SHVITES.
funFact: Switzerland has four official languages (German, French, Italian, Romansh) and four names for itself: Schweiz (German), Suisse (French), Svizzera (Italian), Svizra (Romansh). Their football team reflects this diversity.

name: Türkiye
audioHint: The 'ü' is like the German 'ü' or French 'u' — round your lips as if to say 'oo' but say 'ee'. TURE-kee-yeh. FIFA officially adopted 'Türkiye' in 2022.
funFact: Türkiye (formerly Turkey in English) requested all international organisations use 'Türkiye' as their official name in 2022. The change was accepted by FIFA and the UN. The name derives from the Türk people and '-iye' (land of). The 'ü' has no English equivalent — lips rounded for 'oo', tongue position for 'ee'.
localPronunciation: Türkiye = TURE-kee-yeh (Turkish, the ü is a rounded front vowel)

name: United States
audioHint: yoo-NYE-ted STAYTS — but globally often just 'USA' (yoo-ess-AY). The team is commonly called 'USMNT' (US Men's National Team).
funFact: The USA is a 2026 World Cup host and a genuine dark horse. Their national team has transformed from perennial outsiders to a side with Premier League, La Liga, and Bundesliga talent throughout the squad.

name: Uruguay
audioHint: The 'U' at the start sounds like 'yoor' — YOOR-uh-gwy. In Spanish: oo-roo-GWAY.
funFact: Uruguay won the first two World Cups (1930 and 1950) and remains the smallest country ever to win the tournament. 'La Celeste' (The Sky Blue) are football royalty despite Uruguay's population of only 3.5 million.

name: Venezuela
audioHint: VEN-ih-ZWAY-luh — the 'zuela' sounds like 'ZWAY-luh', not 'ZOO-eh-luh'.
funFact: Venezuela's name means 'Little Venice' — Spanish explorer Amerigo Vespucci thought the indigenous stilt houses reminded him of Venice, Italy. Their football team, known as La Vinotinto (The Wine-Red), have dramatically improved in recent years.

name: Algeria
audioHint: al-JEER-ee-uh — stress on 'JEER'. In Arabic: Al-Jazāʾir (al-juh-ZAH-ir).
funFact: Algeria's Arabic name Al-Jazāʾir means 'The Islands' — referring to islands that once dotted Algiers' harbour, now mostly submerged. Algeria's 2014 World Cup run was their best, reaching the Round of 16.

name: DR Congo
audioHint: dee-ar KON-goh. Also called DRC or Congo-Kinshasa to distinguish from the Republic of Congo. In French: KONG-goh.
funFact: DR Congo (formerly Zaire) was the first sub-Saharan African team to reach a World Cup finals in 1974, losing heavily to Yugoslavia, Scotland, and Brazil — but they were there. Kinshasa is one of Africa's biggest cities.
